title: Introducing Cache Response Rules
summary: Cloudflare's new Cache Response Rules strip cache-blocking headers like Set-Cookie and adjust Cache-Control after the origin responds, so misconfigured origins stop blocking cacheable content.
details: Origin servers often send headers like Set-Cookie or an overly restrictive Cache-Control that make otherwise cacheable content bypass the cache entirely.

The new rules run after the origin's response arrives but before Cloudflare writes it to cache, so they can strip or rewrite headers such as Set-Cookie, ETag, and Cache-Control without touching the origin server.
A cloudflare_only flag lets teams adjust Cloudflare's own caching behavior separately from what headers the visitor's browser sees.

title: The case for a cooldown: Why Dependabot now waits before issuing version updates
summary: GitHub's Dependabot now waits three days by default before opening pull requests for new package versions, giving maintainers and researchers time to catch malicious releases first.
details: Supply-chain attacks exploit how fast automated tooling pulls in new package versions — one compromised npm package spread for about two hours before it was caught.
The three-day cooldown applies only to routine version updates; security patches still arrive immediately, and the delay is configurable per project via a cooldown option in dependabot.yml.
A review of past incidents found malicious versions of packages like axios and ua-parser-js were typically caught within a few hours of publication — well inside the new cooldown window.

title: Cloudflare WAF protects WordPress applications from two high-severity vulnerabilities
summary: Cloudflare rolled out WAF rules blocking two high-severity WordPress bugs — a SQL injection and an unauthenticated remote-code-execution flaw — while sites finish updating to patched versions.
details: One flaw (CVE-2026-63030) lets an unauthenticated attacker execute code via the REST API's batch endpoint when a persistent object cache isn't in use — no login or user interaction needed.
The other (CVE-2026-60137) is a SQL injection affecting WordPress 6.8 and later, where crafted input can alter database queries.
The WAF rules are a stopgap, not a fix — WordPress has released patched versions (6.8.6, 6.9.5, 7.0.2, 7.1 Beta 2), and sites still need to update.

title: Shipyard: How We Built Slack’s Next-Generation EC2 Platform
summary: Slack rebuilt its EC2 platform, Shipyard, to treat servers as immutable, replaceable images instead of continuously patched machines — bringing container-style discipline to raw EC2 instances.
details: Instead of continuously patching long-lived servers, Shipyard bakes a shared 'slack-zero' base image with OS hardening and monitoring, and lets teams build their own AMIs on top of it.

Instances are replaced rather than patched: AWS Instance Refresh and Karpenter cycle out old machines, while a component called 'The Reaper' automatically retires instances past their lifespan or with security issues.
A deployment orchestrator called Gondola runs metric-driven safety checks during rollout and can automatically roll back a change if something looks wrong.

title: How GitHub gave every repository a durable owner
summary: GitHub gave every one of its internal repositories a validated owner, archiving thousands nobody claimed, to fix security workflows that stalled when no one knew who to contact about a repo.
details: Fewer than half of GitHub's 14,000+ internal repositories had clear ownership, which slowed down security work like scanning remediation since nobody knew who to contact.
GitHub tracked ownership with two fields — an ownership type (Service Catalog, Team, or Individual) and a validated owner name — auto-populating about 1,500 repos from its existing service catalog.
An automated job opened 30-day warning issues on unowned repos and archived those left unclaimed, cutting active repositories from 11,000 to about 3,000 in under 45 days.

title: Vercel acquires Better Auth to accelerate open source auth
summary: Vercel is acquiring Better Auth, the open-source TypeScript auth library with 4.7M+ weekly downloads, to build 'Agent Auth' — scoped, revocable identities for AI agents.
details: Better Auth is a framework-agnostic authentication library developers can self-host and own, rather than depending on a hosted auth vendor.
The library stays free and open source under its MIT license, keeps its name, and continues under its existing community governance model — this is a team acquisition, not a shutdown.
A key motivation is Agent Auth: giving individual AI agents their own identity and scoped, revocable permissions instead of having them all act under one shared credential.

title: Introducing the new Vercel Agent
summary: Vercel Agent now lives in your dashboard as an AI assistant that investigates production issues, reviews pull requests, and proposes fixes, but only acts once you approve its plan.
details: The agent works read-only by default and must propose a specific plan before it can touch anything, so it never silently changes production.
It runs under its own identity for auditability and executes generated code in an isolated microVM sandbox, not against live systems directly.
Beyond alert triage, it can trace cost spikes to the code change that caused them and evaluate whether a feature flag is safe to roll out.

title: The cost of saying yes has changed
summary: AI has made writing code cheap, but owning and maintaining it is just as expensive as before, so the real skill now is judging which changes are actually cheap to accept.
details: A change is genuinely cheap only when it avoids touching authorization, product contracts, billing, or anything that creates a new support obligation.
Instead of arguing about scope in meetings, ask for a small constrained attempt with minimal patches and tests, then review the actual diff.
A trivial-looking diff that nobody wants to maintain long-term is still a liability, not a bargain, even if it was fast to generate.

title: How Speechify serves 500,000 dynamic pages to 60 million users on Vercel
summary: Speechify rebuilt its infrastructure on Next.js and Vercel to serve over 500,000 dynamically generated pages in 40+ languages to 60 million users without static generation.
details: Combining Vercel Data Cache, incremental static regeneration, and Next.js Cache Components let pages render once and then serve from the edge until content changes.
Switching to fluid compute for automatic scaling cut infrastructure costs by half while handling hundreds of thousands of daily visitors.
Instant rollbacks and continuous deployment let a small growth team ship every 2-3 days without a dedicated platform engineering team.

title: Open-weight models surge to 29% of volume, price per token flattens
summary: Vercel’s AI Gateway data shows open-weight models jumped from 11% to 29% of token volume in two months, while average price per token stayed flat despite frontier prices rising.
details: Open-weight models now handle 29% of tokens but under 4% of spend, showing enterprises are routing routine work to cheaper models.
Frontier providers still dominate high-stakes work: Anthropic alone takes 61% of spend on just 32% of tokens across coding agents and back-office tasks.
A newly released open-weight model (GLM 5.2) captured 76% of its model family’s tokens within two weeks, showing how fast enterprise adoption can shift.

title: Automating cross-repo documentation with GitHub Agentic Workflows
summary: The GitHub Aspire team built an agentic workflow that automatically drafts documentation pull requests whenever a product feature merges, cutting the usual weeks-long documentation lag.
details: The workflow reads a merged pull request’s diff, decides whether docs are needed, and drafts content directly in the docs repo following existing conventions.
Instead of writing directly, the agent emits structured intent that a separate, permission-scoped handler turns into a real draft PR, keeping humans in control.
The same subject matter expert who reviewed the original code change is automatically assigned to review the generated docs, keeping accuracy high.

title: Unlocking the Cloudflare app ecosystem with OAuth for all
summary: Cloudflare opened self-managed OAuth to all developers, replacing manual partner-only onboarding, after migrating its core OAuth engine from Hydra 1.x to 2.x with zero downtime.
details: Previously only a small set of manually onboarded partners could use real OAuth flows against the Cloudflare API — everyone else fell back to API tokens for delegated access.
The underlying OAuth engine, Hydra, was upgraded from 1.x to 2.x using a blue-green deployment, with schema migrations rewritten as non-locking operations to avoid downtime on live tables.
The upgrade cut API P95 latency by 45% (185ms to 101ms) and CPU usage by 37%, while the live cutover itself moved over 130 million database rows in about three hours.

title: Copilot vs. raw API access: What are you actually paying for?
summary: Now that Copilot bills at listed API rates, the real choice isn't price — it's GitHub's integrated workflow and token-efficient harness versus building your own stack.
details: GitHub's internal harness reached similar task-resolution results while using fewer tokens than raw API calls across benchmarks like SWE-bench Verified and TerminalBench 2.0.
Copilot adds things raw APIs don't: org-wide policy controls, pooled AI credits, usage dashboards, and a bring-your-own-key option to use that workflow with an external provider's billing.
Raw API access still makes sense if you need full control over prompts, retrieval, routing, logs, and security — infrastructure Copilot's harness otherwise handles for you.

title: Next chapter: Restructuring GitHub’s bug bounty program
summary: GitHub is restructuring its bug bounty program: lower flat public payouts, a new invite-only VIP tier that pays far more, and stricter rules meant to cut low-effort reports.
details: A new permanent, invite-only VIP program pays much more (up to $30,000+ for critical findings) to researchers who've already proven a track record of solid reports.
The public program's payouts are now flat and lower than before — for example $250 for low severity and $10,000 for critical, down from the previous variable scale.
New researchers must clear a HackerOne signal threshold; those below it get four initial submissions to build a track record before limits apply. Changes take effect July 27, 2026.

title: Better tools made Copilot code review worse. Here’s how we actually improved it.
summary: Switching Copilot code review to the CLI's grep/glob/view tools first made reviews worse, until GitHub rewrote its prompts to stop the agent browsing the whole repo.
details: The problem wasn't the tools themselves — it was that the agent used them to browse the whole repository instead of investigating just the pull request's changes.
GitHub rewrote the tool instructions so the agent anchors to the diff first, narrows with grep/glob before opening files, and reads only the specific line ranges it needs.
Those prompt changes cut the average review cost by roughly 20% without touching the underlying tools or hurting review quality.

title: Run any Dockerfile on Vercel
summary: Vercel now runs any Dockerfile-based service — Go, Rails, Spring Boot, Express, and more — as a first-class deployment with autoscaling and pay-per-use pricing.
details: Any HTTP service that listens on the $PORT environment variable can deploy from a Dockerfile.vercel file, with no local daemon, registry, or cluster to manage.
Containers get the same infrastructure as Vercel's other deployments: automatic preview URLs per commit, traffic-based autoscaling, and unified logs, traces, and metrics.
Billing is for actual CPU execution time rather than idle time, and optimized boot images let containers start quickly despite running in a full container.

title: Vercel Services: Run full stack on Vercel
summary: Vercel Services, now in public beta, lets a frontend and a backend like FastAPI or Express live in one project that deploys and rolls back together.
details: Frontend, backend, and other services in a project deploy and roll back atomically, with a shared preview environment so a full change set can be reviewed together.
It supports FastAPI, Flask, Express, Hono, Go, and Rust with zero-config deployment, plus internal service-to-service communication that never touches the public internet.
It bundles what full-stack apps need — databases from Neon, Supabase, or AWS, Blob storage, background queues/workflows/cron, and short-lived credentials instead of stored secrets.

title: Build realtime voice agents on AI Gateway
summary: Vercel added voice to its AI Gateway, letting developers add realtime voice conversations, text-to-speech, and speech-to-text using the same API calls they already use for text and image.
details: Realtime voice supports low-latency two-way conversation where a user can interrupt the model mid-response, useful for voice assistants.
A single useRealtime hook in AI SDK 7 manages the WebSocket connection and microphone capture, with authentication handled server-side so API keys never reach the browser.
All three audio modes get the same provider routing, spend controls, and observability as Gateway’s existing text, image, and video traffic.

title: How to build interactive experiences with canvases
summary: GitHub Copilot's new canvases turn chat into a live, clickable workspace — a /create-canvas command builds interfaces like an issue triage board or a codebase diagram.
details: Canvases are custom interactive interfaces the agent builds and updates live, not just chat — you click, drag, and filter instead of only typing prompts.
Examples GitHub built include a swipeable issue-triage card, a draggable codebase architecture diagram, and a cleanup view for stale Copilot worktree sessions.
Other canvases coach you on writing better prompts, or search Slack, Teams, email, and docs to find who has expertise on a given file or topic.

title: How the 2026 World Cup affected Internet traffic
summary: Cloudflare's 2026 World Cup traffic data shows late-night kickoffs more than doubled some countries' internet traffic, while halftime produced spikes or dips depending on how each country watches.
details: Matches with midnight-to-8am kickoffs pushed some countries' traffic to more than double their normal level; daytime matches barely moved traffic since people were already online.
Argentina vs. Switzerland was the single most traffic-moving match (1.26x), and Argentina generated the strongest traffic bump of any team across all of its matches (1.17x).
44 countries saw traffic rise during halftime and hydration breaks as fans checked their phones, while 8 countries that pause video streams instead saw traffic dip.

title: How Searchable ships customer-requested features in 30 minutes on Vercel
summary: Searchable, which tracks how brands show up in AI search tools, uses Vercel's AI Gateway to ship customer-requested features in about 30 minutes without swapping keys or SDKs.
details: Searchable monitors brand visibility across AI search engines like ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Claude, and gives guidance on how to improve it.
Centralizing model access through Vercel's AI Gateway let them drop manual SDK integration and key management, cutting time-to-ship for a requested feature to about 30 minutes.
The team reports a 5x increase in development velocity while processing over 100 billion tokens, and can test different AI models without rotating API keys.

title: Vercel and Shopify are rebuilding Hydrogen
summary: Vercel and Shopify are rebuilding Hydrogen from scratch so headless storefronts are no longer locked into one platform, betting on a more open, portable web stack.
details: The new Hydrogen splits into a core layer of shared Shopify API utilities, so developers stop rewriting the same glue code like money formatting by hand.
A client layer ships pre-built state management and cart/form handling, removing the need to reimplement common storefront logic every time.
The server layer runs on frameworks like Next.js, Nuxt, and SvelteKit instead of a proprietary runtime, so a store can run anywhere JavaScript does.

title: How we built saga rollbacks for Cloudflare Workflows
summary: Cloudflare Workflows now supports the saga pattern, letting developers attach a rollback function to each step so a failed multi-step process can automatically undo what already succeeded.
details: Each step.do() call can take a rollback function that runs only if the overall workflow ultimately fails, receiving that step’s original output.
Rollbacks run in reverse order of execution and rely on persistent step history to know exactly which steps actually completed.
This removes the need for developers to hand-write compensation logic to track successes and failures outside of the step definitions themselves.

title: Teaching agents product design at Vercel
summary: Vercel built a system of agent instructions, automated linters, and a feedback-gathering workflow so coding agents can learn why its UI patterns exist, not just copy them.
details: Coding agents can copy a codebase's visual style but can't tell why a pattern exists, because that reasoning lives in design reviews and Slack threads, not in the code itself.

Vercel splits enforcement into two layers: linters that catch deterministic rule violations automatically (like nested modals), and an agent skill that supplies judgment-based context for less mechanical decisions.

A feedback-gathering workflow pulls input from Slack, Figma, and GitHub to keep guidelines current, and the system is tested with before/after fixtures to check whether agents actually generalize guidance to new interfaces.

title: Content Independence Day, one year on: building the business model for the agentic Internet
summary: Cloudflare’s one-year report on the "agentic internet": non-human traffic has passed half of all internet traffic, and a market for licensing content to AI is starting to form.
details: AI training crawlers jumped from 22% to 52% of crawler requests between spring 2025 and June 2026 — the crawl mix has flipped toward training.
Roughly 2.5 billion people now use generative AI regularly, a platform shift reportedly moving about twice as fast as smartphone adoption.
Over 50 publisher–AI licensing deals since 2023 show content has real economic value, though the terms are still bespoke rather than standardized.

title: Unmasking the crawls with Attribution Business Insights
summary: Cloudflare added a dashboard showing website owners how AI crawlers behave — bot-vs-human traffic, and how many times each crawler visits per referral it sends back.
details: Crawl-to-referral ratios run from 118:1 up to nearly 50,000:1, so AI crawlers take far more than they send back compared with traditional search.
Traffic is broken down by operator and crawlers are classified as training, search, or agent, with volume and bandwidth per bot.
It is meant to inform licensing negotiations and sits alongside — not inside — the existing bot-management rules.

title: GenPage: Towards End-to-End Generative Homepage Construction at Netflix
summary: Netflix built a single generative transformer that constructs a personalized homepage end-to-end, replacing its traditional multi-stage recommendation pipeline.
details: Custom tokens mapped to Netflix rows and titles, instead of a generic text tokenizer, cut sequence length ~75% and let business rules be enforced by masking tokens.
Enriching the user-context prompt cut loss ~6.9%, while scaling the model from 120M to 900M parameters gained only ~1.3% — representation mattered more than raw size.
Generating only the first few high-attention titles per row, then scoring the rest in one pass, cut end-to-end latency ~20% versus the production baseline.

title: Vercel Open Source Program: Spring 2026 cohort
summary: Vercel named the 33 open-source projects in its Spring 2026 funding cohort, each getting platform credits and support — ecosystem news rather than a technical deep-dive.
details: Each of the 33 selected projects receives $3,600 in Vercel platform credits plus support.
Selection spans frameworks, libraries, and developer tooling across the open-source ecosystem.
The "Spring 2026 cohort" framing points to a recurring, cohort-based program rather than a one-off grant.

title: How we used DSPy to turn AI evaluations into better responses in Dash chat
summary: Dropbox built an automated feedback loop with DSPy: calibrate LLM judges against human labels, then use those judges to optimize its Dash chat agent’s prompts.
details: Scoring the agent across separate dimensions — intent, tool use, context selection, grounding, task completion — shows where failures happen instead of one blunt score.
Human-labeled rubrics come first; DSPy’s GEPA and MIPROv2 then optimize toward agreement with humans, not an abstract quality metric.
Prompt edits were kept small and reviewed, doubling experiment velocity while guarding against runaway optimization in production.

title: AI SDK 7
summary: Vercel’s AI SDK 7 targets production agents: a unified reasoning option across providers, durable workflows that survive restarts, and built-in OpenTelemetry observability.
details: A single reasoning option and typed tool/runtime contexts replace provider-specific reasoning APIs and unsafe credential passing.
A new workflow package lets agents persist state across steps and survive process restarts and deploys — closing a real production gap.
Redesigned telemetry with OpenTelemetry and per-step stats gives visibility into agent behavior without wiring callbacks everywhere.

title: Introducing Meerkat: an experiment in global consensus
summary: Cloudflare is testing a new consensus algorithm called QuePaxa in a service called Meerkat, aiming to keep control-plane data consistent across data centers without a single leader node.
details: Leader-based consensus systems like Raft can stall or become unavailable when the leader fails or the network degrades, which is a bigger risk across globally distributed data centers.
QuePaxa removes the need for a single leader, letting concurrent proposals cooperate instead of triggering disruptive re-election pauses.
In tests under adversarial network conditions, Meerkat sustained roughly 10x the throughput of Raft and Multi-Paxos, showing the consensus algorithm choice matters for real-world resilience.

title: Cloudflare proudly joins the UK government's Cyber Resilience Pledge
summary: Cloudflare became a founding signatory of the UK government's Cyber Resilience Pledge, a voluntary commitment to board-level security oversight, baseline protections, and vetted suppliers.
details: The pledge asks organizations to commit to board-level accountability for cybersecurity rather than leaving it solely to technical teams.
Cloudflare ties its participation to concrete practices, such as requiring critical suppliers to hold ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II certifications.
The announcement cites UK-specific risk data, including that 43% of surveyed British businesses reported a cyber incident in the past year, as context for why pledges like this matter.

title: Your Worker can now have its own cache in front of it
summary: Cloudflare launched Workers Cache, letting a Worker cache its own responses in front of it using standard HTTP headers instead of separate CDN configuration.
details: Enabling the cache is a one-line change in the Wrangler config, and caching behavior is then controlled with familiar Cache-Control and Vary headers.
The cache is two-tiered: a nearby regional tier is checked first, then a shared upper tier, so once any location fills the cache, other regions can hit it too.
Because ctx.props can scope cache keys per user, developers can cache responses from authenticated, multi-tenant APIs without leaking one user's data to another.

title: Making AI search smarter
summary: Cloudflare is piloting ways to pay creators when AI systems use their content in search answers, instead of only rewarding pages that get crawled.
details: AI-generated search summaries sharply cut referral traffic to original sources, with one cited study finding users click through only about 8% of the time when a summary is shown.
Cloudflare says over half of the crawl traffic it sees hits pages that haven't actually changed, so its new signal-based approach aims to cut wasted crawling rather than just block it.
A 'pay-per-query' pilot with partners like Ceramic.ai would compensate publishers when their content is actually used in an answer, not just when a bot crawls it.

title: Your site, your rules: new AI traffic options for all customers
summary: Cloudflare now lets site owners separately allow or block three kinds of AI bots (search, agent, and training crawlers) instead of one all-or-nothing switch.
details: Site owners can now distinguish bots that index content for search, bots acting on a person's behalf in real time, and bots scraping content to train models.
Starting September 15, 2026, new domains will block Training and Agent bots on ad-monetized pages by default, while Search bots stay allowed, unless the owner opts out.
A new optional robots.txt signal lets sites specify how their content may be used, ranging from no storage at all to full summarization and reproduction.

title: Announcing the Monetization Gateway: charge for any resource behind Cloudflare via x402
summary: Cloudflare opened a waitlist for Monetization Gateway, letting sites charge small stablecoin payments per request for pages, APIs, or datasets using the x402 protocol.
details: The flow rides on HTTP's existing 402 Payment Required status code: a client requests a resource, gets a price back, pays, and resubmits the request with proof of payment.
Payments settle peer-to-peer in stablecoins in under a second, a design aimed at AI agents that read a resource once rather than maintain a subscription.
No account signup is required from the payer; Cloudflare's model treats the payment itself as the credential that unlocks the resource.

title: A broken DNSSEC rollover took down .al. Now 1.1.1.1 tells you when validation is bypassed
summary: When Albania’s .al domain broke from a botched DNSSEC rollover, Cloudflare’s 1.1.1.1 kept it reachable but now returns an error code so clients can see DNS security was bypassed.
details: Restoring a broken DNSSEC domain via a Negative Trust Anchor used to be silent — clients could not tell a bypassed answer from a spoofed one.
The new Extended DNS Error 33 signals in the response itself that a Negative Trust Anchor was applied, giving operators direct visibility.
Tooling is adopting it — kdig names EDE 33 and an Unbound pull request is in review — so the transparency spreads across the ecosystem.

title: Building Service Topology at Scale: Architecture, Challenges, and Lessons Learned
summary: Netflix built a real-time system that maps which services talk to which across its infrastructure, processing millions of network flow records per second instead of batching them hourly.
details: Continuous streaming with backpressure slows the pipeline to a sustainable rate under a spike or GC pause instead of collapsing — an advantage batching does not give.
A three-stage pipeline with multiple redistribution points spread load when some instances saw 100x the traffic of others; one design choice solved several problems.
They dropped immutable data structures on the hot path — a Scala norm — after profiling showed GC costing more CPU than the real work. Measure before assuming.

title: Introducing Precursor: detecting agentic behavior with continuous client-side signals
summary: Cloudflare’s Precursor detects bots by watching how a visitor behaves across a whole session, rather than judging them at a single checkpoint like a CAPTCHA.
details: Consistent human behavior over time is hard to fake, so session-long analysis catches bots that can pass an individual challenge.
Because signals accumulate across the session, refreshing the page or restarting a challenge does not reset detection.
It collects the minimum useful signal — keystroke timing, not the keys themselves — kept internal and not tied to a user’s identity.

title: Improving Smart Tiered Cache for Public Cloud Regions
summary: Cloudflare fixed a case where caching in front of anycast cloud origins sent traffic across continents, by letting customers name their cloud region so it picks the right upper-tier data center.
details: Anycast origins on AWS/GCP/Azure could hairpin traffic — an Asian request routed to Chicago to fetch from Singapore — adding hundreds of milliseconds.
A cloud region hint like aws:us-east-1, set via dashboard, API, or Terraform, tells Cloudflare which upper tier is genuinely closest.
It matches origin IPs against provider range files and tier data refreshed every 15 minutes, with geographic fallback until real latency data builds up.
